title: The Cones Of Dunshire

jokes.text: The last time he was in between jobs, he got deep into claymation, so this should be different, hopefully.
jokes.setup: Ben has been fired and is cooking up something big during his week off
jokes.punchline: Leslie's dry acknowledgment that his last unemployment hobby was claymation with skeptical hope this will be different
jokes.explanation: Strong character-based comedy showing Leslie's familiarity with Ben's unemployment quirks. The specificity of claymation makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So what are you working on during your week off?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm thinking about starting a consulting firm. Help small businesses with their finances and operations.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's great. Last time you had a week off, you made that claymation movie about a snail.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That was a really good snail, Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It was. But I'm hoping this is different.

jokes.text: Presenting 'The Cones of Dunshire,' a brand-new gaming experience. 8 to 12 players.
jokes.setup: Ben's been working on something big during unemployment
jokes.punchline: It's an overly complex board game with a ridiculous name and variable player count
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - Ben would absolutely create an absurdly complex board game. 'Cones of Dunshire' became iconic and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, I've been working on something during my unemployment. It's a board game.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It's called 'Cones of Dunshire.'

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It has a variable player count of two to four players, but theoretically up to six with the expansion pack I'm working on.

jokes.text: Two wizards, a Maverick, the arbiter, two warriors, a corporal, and a ledgerman. Now, the ledgerman just keeps score, and he wears this hat.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining the complex player roles in his board game
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity that the ledgerman has a special hat just for keeping score
jokes.explanation: Builds on the game's absurd complexity. The hat detail is perfect Ben-like over-engineering.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The ledgerman has a special hat just for keeping score.

jokes.text: Oh, boy.
jokes.setup: Ben's enthusiastic explanation of the ledgerman's hat
jokes.punchline: Leslie's understated reaction showing she knows this is getting out of hand
jokes.explanation: Perfect economic reaction. Two words that convey Leslie's dawning realization of Ben's obsession.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The ledgerman's hat is actually a crucial part of the ensemble. You see, the brim helps shield the eyes from the sun while you're reviewing the ledger, and the crown provides optimal ventilation for extended periods of accounting work.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, there is no ledgerman's hat.

jokes.text: Ben's stream-of-consciousness explanation getting increasingly convoluted about challenge plays, trivia cards, roadblocks, and rolling dice to see how many dice to roll
jokes.setup: Ben trying to explain his game logically
jokes.punchline: The explanation becomes circular and incomprehensible, including rolling dice to determine dice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant sustained cringe comedy. Ben's inability to explain his own game reveals his over-engineering tendencies.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, so the way the game works is you roll the dice to determine what you do, and then you roll other dice to determine the outcome of that action.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: But then you need to roll dice to determine which dice you actually rolled, so you roll a die to see what the first die was supposed to be.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: And if that die contradicts the other die, you roll another die to determine which die is correct.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So basically you're just rolling dice to figure out what dice you rolled. It's elegant, really.

jokes.text: Are the cones a metaphor? Well, yes and no.
jokes.setup: Natural question about what the cones represent
jokes.punchline: Ben's non-answer that somehow makes it more confusing
jokes.explanation: Perfect pseudo-intellectual response. Ben thinks he's being profound while being completely unhelpful.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: They're traffic cones.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What do you think they represent?

jokes.text: Oh, my God, the Maverick should be able to trade lumber for agriculture credits. How have I not thought of this before?
jokes.setup: Ben explaining his already overly complex game
jokes.punchline: He realizes he needs to add MORE complexity while explaining it
jokes.explanation: Shows Ben's compulsive need to perfect things even mid-explanation. Very authentic character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So you roll the dice, move your piece around the board, collect resource cards based on where you land, then you trade those cards with other players to build structures, but only if you have the required combination and you're on a designated trading post, which rotates every three turns...

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. This needs something. It's not complicated enough.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, now every resource card has a secondary action value on the back, so when you're NOT using it for trading, you can flip it over and use it as a modifier for your movement roll, but only on even-numbered turns, and only if you're wearing the ceremonial sash I'm going to add.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Perfect. That's perfect.

jokes.text: This is nothing, right? When do you go back to work again? Tomorrow. It's fine. I'll just throw this in the garbage.
jokes.setup: Ben has spent a week creating this elaborate game
jokes.punchline: He's ready to immediately trash it when reality hits about returning to work
jokes.explanation: Relatable cringe moment. Ben's quick pivot from pride to disposal shows his insecurity.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I spent a week on this game and I'm ready to throw it in the trash the second we have to go back to work.

jokes.text: The voters, to use a technical political term, gave you the stanky boot.
jokes.setup: Jamm explaining why Leslie can't make decisions anymore
jokes.punchline: 'Stanky boot' as a fake technical political term
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor. Jamm would definitely think 'stanky boot' sounds official.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Leslie can't make decisions anymore because she has what we in politics call 'Stanky boot.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's not a real thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: It's a very real condition. Look it up.

jokes.text: What, you think I'm gonna use it to put a new limestone shower in my office? Yeah, now I do.
jokes.setup: Jamm rhetorically asking if Leslie thinks he'd misuse funds
jokes.punchline: Leslie's immediate confirmation that yes, now she does think that
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline structure. Jamm's specific luxury item makes it more absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: You think I'd misuse funds?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, not at all.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But now that you mention it... yes. Yes, I do.

jokes.text: For sale. Small house. Location: Forest. It's a little wordy, don't you think?
jokes.setup: Ron's minimalist real estate listing
jokes.punchline: Tom sarcastically suggesting the bare-bones description is too wordy
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between Ron's minimalism and Tom's marketing instincts. Great character-based humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Three-bedroom house. Plenty of land. Wood inside.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, I think your listing might be a little too detailed. Maybe try shortening it?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: House.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: There we go. That's perfect.

jokes.text: Current owner will not clean up shotgun shells or animal carcasses. Fine, I'll clean up the shotgun shells, but the carcasses remain to fertilize my berry patch.
jokes.setup: Ron's brutally honest listing details
jokes.punchline: His compromise of cleaning shells but keeping dead animals for practical reasons
jokes.explanation: Pure Ron Swanson logic. The practical justification for keeping carcasses is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've listed my house for sale. Here are some of the listing details I've included.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Large lot. Room for a man to work. Dead animals for practical purposes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Some might say the dead animals are a drawback. I disagree.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: As a compromise, I've agreed to have the shells cleaned. But the animals stay.

jokes.text: Fine, I'll clean up the shotgun shells, but the carcasses remain to fertilize my berry patch.
jokes.setup: Tom's horror at Ron's listing details
jokes.punchline: Ron's 'compromise' of keeping the carcasses for practical gardening purposes
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that shows Ron's twisted practicality. The berry patch detail makes it more absurd and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've removed the bodies from the property, as requested. They're in a storage unit now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, what? No! You can't just move them!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm willing to compromise. I'll keep the skeletons for composting purposes. Excellent for the garden.

jokes.text: Spladow! Check it. 'Regal Meagle Realty.' 'Find your castle.'
jokes.setup: Donna revealing her real estate business
jokes.punchline: The alliterative business name and slogan playing on her last name
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Donna. The business name is clever but not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I've started my own real estate business. It's called Donna Meagle's Marvelous Meagle Mansions.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: My slogan is: 'Donna Meagle's Marvelous Meagle Mansions: Making Magical Memories in Meagle Manner.'

jokes.text: The question I always ask myself is, what kind of mogul should I be? Fashion mogul, energy drink mogul. I even thought about downhill skiing. A mogul mogul.
jokes.setup: Tom considering different business mogul types
jokes.punchline: The skiing mogul/business mogul pun
jokes.explanation: Decent pun that fits Tom's entrepreneurial delusions, but it's a bit forced.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I want to be a business mogul. You know, like a skiing mogul, except with business.

jokes.text: A little too good. Let's see if you live up to the hype. I'm not joking.
jokes.setup: Ben's new colleague Frank has heard good things about him
jokes.punchline: Frank's intimidating clarification that he's serious about the pressure
jokes.explanation: Mild workplace comedy. Frank's intensity creates uncomfortable tension but isn't particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I've heard great things about you, Ben. People say you're smart, you're dedicated, you're a hard worker. So I'm going to put a lot of pressure on you, and I hope you can handle it.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Thanks, I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I'm serious. A lot of pressure.

jokes.text: I hope you were speaking to her in a calming voice 'cause babies can hear everything. Baby, if you can still hear me, I love you. The phone is hung up.
jokes.setup: Leslie finishing a phone call with Ann who's pregnant
jokes.punchline: Chris's concern leading to Leslie talking to a disconnected phone
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's devotion to Ann and Chris's earnestness about pregnancy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, Ann, I'm so excited for you. I'll call you tomorrow. Bye!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Leslie, is Ann okay? I heard she was pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: She's fine, Chris. She's really happy about it.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great. I was worried she might be in some kind of distress.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, everything's perfect. She's going to be a great mom.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Well, if there's anything I can do to help, just let me know.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, that's really sweet of you, Chris. I'll definitely—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, did you hear that? Chris said he'd help! Ann? Hello?

jokes.text: Ever since I started city council, I have gone head-to-head with Jamm 112 times. He has won 56 times, I have won 56 times. I will not lose our last battle.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her conflict with Jamm
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific scorekeeping showing her competitive obsession
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie Knope behavior. The precise tracking and 56-56 tie is beautifully obsessive.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jeremy Jamm has blocked 47 of my initiatives. I've blocked 3 of his. But here's the thing - two of those were just to block him, and one was actually a good initiative that I would've supported if he hadn't supported it first. So really, I've blocked 2 of his initiatives. But I'm still winning because I blocked them more recently, which means I have momentum.

jokes.text: Maybe I can mediate, like Dr. Phil, only qualified.
jokes.setup: Chris offers to help mediate between Leslie and Jamm
jokes.punchline: His subtle dig at Dr. Phil's qualifications while comparing himself
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - three words 'only qualified' deliver a perfect subtle burn on Dr. Phil while showing Chris's confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I have a master's degree in public policy from Georgetown. Dr. Phil got his Ph.D. in clinical psychology from a diploma mill in Oklahoma. So I'm basically more qualified than him.

jokes.text: I just can't justify owning four cabins. Two cabins.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining why he's selling his cabin
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that he owns multiple cabins, immediately downplayed
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy. Ron's wealth reveal followed by immediate correction shows his secretive nature perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm selling my cabin.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Well, one of my cabins.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have several.

jokes.text: Plus, it doesn't have these razor-sharp perimeter defense planks sticking out of the ground.
jokes.setup: Ron describing the advantages of his new cabin
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of dangerous security measures at his current cabin
jokes.explanation: Pure Ron paranoia. The casual mention of literal defensive spikes is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: My new cabin has several advantages over my current one. Better insulation, more efficient wood stove, and I won't have to replace the bear traps every season.

jokes.text: Ron, you want to stay outside and do nothing while they make your cabin look like garbage? I'd like that very much. We can watch the shadows get longer.
jokes.setup: Tom asking if Ron wants to avoid the staging process
jokes.punchline: Ron's enthusiasm for literally watching shadows move
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson zen moment. Finding joy in watching shadows is beautifully minimalist.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So you want to skip the staging process?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't want to skip anything. I want to stand very still and watch the shadows move across the walls.

jokes.text: If I'm murdered tonight, start the investigation with those two guys.
jokes.setup: Ben's new coworkers are acting suspiciously excited about his case-solving abilities
jokes.punchline: His genuine fear that they might kill him for being too good at his job
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor. Ben's paranoia about workplace competition is relatable and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Why is everyone being so nice to me? Why are they so interested in my cases? Are they... are they going to kill me?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm too good at this. I solve cases too efficiently. They see me as a threat.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I need to start making mistakes. Intentional mistakes. Or I'm a dead man.

jokes.text: No shoes, you lose. That means we should have shoes then, right? No shoes...
jokes.setup: Jamm's house rule about removing shoes
jokes.punchline: Leslie's logical confusion about the contradictory phrasing
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay humor. Leslie's literal interpretation of Jamm's unclear rule is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: No shoes in the house. That's the rule.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But you're wearing shoes right now.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I live here.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So the rule is... no shoes in the house, except for you, who live here?

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not a rule. That's just you describing what you're doing right now.

jokes.text: It's not sexual. I'm just a really big fan of her game. Because in that picture, she's wearing a bikini. Yeah, thanks. Photoshopped it myself.
jokes.setup: Jamm defending a photo of Michelle Wie
jokes.punchline: His admission that he photoshopped her into a bikini completely undermining his 'not sexual' claim
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy. Jamm's complete lack of self-awareness about the contradiction is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: This is not sexual. I'm just a fan of golf.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Then why is she in a bikini?

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: I photoshopped that.

jokes.text: Got ourselves a sleeping bag, a giant tin of caramel corn, and an irregular skirt from DKNY. The hem is crooked, but it was 8 bucks.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing supplies for their overnight negotiation
jokes.punchline: The random inclusion of a discounted designer skirt with specific flaw details
jokes.explanation: Good character detail. Leslie's specific bargain-hunting even during political negotiations is very her.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, we have sleeping bags, we have snacks, we have a first aid kit, we have a discounted designer skirt with a small stain on the hem and a broken zipper.

jokes.text: How sweet is this table, by the way, huh? I got it from an actual benihana. Cost me 4 grand. Worth every penny. Hai!
jokes.setup: Jamm showing off his dining setup
jokes.punchline: His pride in an overpriced restaurant table and awkward Japanese exclamation
jokes.explanation: Typical Jamm tackiness. Shows his poor taste and cultural insensitivity.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Look at this table I got! Cost me eight thousand dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: It's from this exclusive Japanese designer. Hideous, right?

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: But that's what makes it perfect. BANZAI!

jokes.text: Can I interest anyone in an authentic a-Japanese a-break-a-fast? Scrambled eggs eaten with chopsticks?
jokes.setup: Jamm trying to be a good host
jokes.punchline: His terrible fake Japanese accent and completely non-Japanese breakfast
jokes.explanation: Predictable cultural insensitivity humor. Fits Jamm's character but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Welcome to my home! I've prepared an authentic Japanese breakfast experience for you.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: I present to you... *attempts exaggerated accent* ...the traditional Japanese morning feast of eggs, bacon, and toast!

jokes.text: This place is so random. I don't know. It's kind of, like, in the middle of nowhere. I know. That's, like, the hilarious part. It's like a joke how long it takes to get here. It's like a meta-cabin.
jokes.setup: Young hipster buyers looking at Ron's cabin
jokes.punchline: They ironically appreciate the inconvenience and call it a 'meta-cabin'
jokes.explanation: Good satire of hipster culture finding meaning in inconvenience. 'Meta-cabin' is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Hipster Buyer 1
dialogue.line: Oh wow, no electricity? That's so... authentic.

dialogue.speaker: Hipster Buyer 2
dialogue.line: And the outhouse is literally fifty yards away? That's peak meta-cabin.

dialogue.speaker: Hipster Buyer 1
dialogue.line: The inconvenience is actually the whole aesthetic. It's ironic, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Hipster Buyer 2
dialogue.line: Exactly. We'll take it. This is the most meta-cabin ever.

jokes.text: I heard that Dave Eggers wrote a short story about this tape cassette that he found under that tree over there. Oh, my God, I heard that Neko Case and Ben Gibbard made out here once.
jokes.setup: Tom trying to increase the cabin's value
jokes.punchline: Making up completely fake indie culture connections to appeal to hipster buyers
jokes.explanation: Smart salesmanship from Tom. The specific indie references show his marketing savvy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This cabin? Totally owned by an obscure folk musician in the '70s. Very indie. Very underground.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: The previous owner? He inspired Bon Iver. Well, not directly inspired, but they definitely shared a similar energy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And see this water stain? That's where he spilled artisanal kombucha. Vintage kombucha. Before it was cool.

dialogue.speaker: Potential buyers
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Absolutely. You basically can't get more authentic than this place.

jokes.text: What if we dissolve the trust? Yes! You just cracked the case. I'm just doing my job.
jokes.setup: Ben solving a complex legal case at his new job
jokes.punchline: His colleagues' over-the-top excitement about a routine solution
jokes.explanation: Mild workplace comedy about overeager colleagues. Ben's modesty contrasts with their excitement.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, so I've reviewed the contract terms, and the solution is actually straightforward. We just need to add a severability clause.

dialogue.speaker: Coworker 1
dialogue.line: Wait, what? You solved it?

dialogue.speaker: Coworker 2
dialogue.line: BEN SOLVED IT! BEN SOLVED IT!

dialogue.speaker: Coworker 1
dialogue.line: This is the greatest day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Coworker 3
dialogue.line: I'm calling my mother! Ben from Parks solved our legal issue!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It's really not that—

dialogue.speaker: Coworker 2
dialogue.line: SEVERABILITY CLAUSE! SEVERABILITY CLAUSE!

jokes.text: You eat the shells, and you throw the seeds out. Wow. You have really immersed yourself in Asian culture.
jokes.setup: Jamm explaining how to eat edamame completely wrong
jokes.punchline: Chris's polite response that highlights how wrong Jamm is about Japanese culture
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction. Chris's diplomatic sarcasm and Jamm's complete ignorance work well together.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: You take the pod, you crack it open with your teeth, and then you swallow the whole thing. Pod and all. That's how they do it in Japan.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm pretty sure you're supposed to squeeze the bean out with your teeth and just eat the bean.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: No, no, no. The pod is the best part. It's got all the fiber.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Right, but it's not edible. The pod is not meant to be eaten.

jokes.text: Ugh, Lezzie. Come on, I'm getting bored.
jokes.setup: Long negotiation session
jokes.punchline: Jamm's inappropriate nickname for Leslie showing his immaturity
jokes.explanation: Establishes Jamm's crudeness but isn't particularly clever or funny, just uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: You know what? Let's call Leslie 'Les-lie... the Lesion.' Get it? Because she's like an infection on this town council.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no, no. I'm Sandra Dee. That's more of my register. You're Zuko.
jokes.setup: Leslie suggesting they sing a Grease duet
jokes.punchline: Jamm insisting on playing the female role because of his vocal range
jokes.explanation: Unexpected character moment. Jamm's musical preferences reveal a softer side while staying true to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: We could do a Grease duet! You could be Danny Zuko.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm going to play Sandy. My vocal range is much better suited for the female part.

jokes.text: I would like to buy five DVDs of this. No, 20. No, 60! No, that's insane. 20.
jokes.setup: Ron watching Leslie and Jamm sing karaoke
jokes.punchline: His escalating enthusiasm for purchasing the performance followed by self-correction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment. His rare display of emotion followed by practical self-correction is beautifully in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I would pay $200 for this to never happen again.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Actually, I would pay $500.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No wait, $1,000. I would pay $1,000 to make this stop.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Scratch that. I would pay any amount of money. Name it. I will write you a check right now.

jokes.text: Glamping is 'glamour camping.' Heated tents, catered meals, wi-fi-- You're describing a hotel.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining glamping to Ron
jokes.punchline: Ron's blunt observation that glamping is just a hotel with extra steps
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy and perfect Ron logic. Four words that completely deflate the glamping concept.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Glamping is like camping, but with luxury amenities. You know, comfortable beds, heated tents, gourmet meals...

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: So it's a hotel.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, no, it's different. You're still outside in nature.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's a hotel with extra steps.

jokes.text: That's part of the charm of this whole place--the cantankerous old coot who owns it. It's authentic!
jokes.setup: Ron rudely closing the open house
jokes.punchline: Tom spinning Ron's antisocial behavior as a selling point
jokes.explanation: Good salesmanship from Tom. Turning Ron's personality flaws into authentic rustic experience.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, what you just witnessed is authentic Ron Swanson. He doesn't do small talk, he doesn't do pleasantries, and he certainly doesn't do open houses. But that's what makes this property special. This is a man's home. A real man's home. Built on a foundation of solitude, self-reliance, and a complete disregard for your feelings.

jokes.text: You are my Nemesis. You're the Superman to my Lex Luthor. You want to be Lex Luthor? Uh, yeah. Lex Luthor is rich.
jokes.setup: Jamm comparing their rivalry to superhero dynamics
jokes.punchline: His practical reasoning for choosing the villain role based on wealth
jokes.explanation: Good character logic from Jamm. His materialistic reasoning for preferring the villain role is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: You know what? I've decided. In our little superhero dynamic, you're the hero and I'm the villain.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Really? Why would you choose to be the villain?

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Because villains are rich. Heroes are always broke, living in studios, driving used cars. Villains have lairs, henchmen, offshore accounts. It's the smart choice.

jokes.text: You don't make sushi rolls out of tuna salad-- Maybe you don't.
jokes.setup: Leslie criticizing Jamm's fake Japanese food
jokes.punchline: Jamm's defensive response implying Leslie lacks culinary creativity
jokes.explanation: Clean comeback structure. Jamm's refusal to admit he's wrong about sushi is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This isn't even real Japanese food, Jeremy. It's just regular American food with soy sauce dumped all over it.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Well maybe if you had any culinary creativity, you'd appreciate what I'm trying to do here.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Culinary creativity? You put sriracha on a hot dog and called it 'Asian fusion.'

jokes.text: Ann arbor sounds disgusting.
jokes.setup: Learning Ann is moving to Ann Arbor
jokes.punchline: Leslie's petty criticism of the city name
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's emotional defensiveness through petty name-calling.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann Arbor? Really? That's just Ann's name twice. It's like if I moved to a city called Leslie Leslie. Which, okay, would be amazing, but that's not the point. Ann Arbor is basically admitting they have no imagination. They saw Ann and thought, 'You know what this needs? More Ann.'

jokes.text: I mean, the stupid state is split up into two pieces! It's ridiculous.
jokes.setup: Leslie ranting about Michigan
jokes.punchline: Her complaint about Michigan's geographical shape as if it's personally offensive
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Leslie's anti-Michigan rant. The geographical complaint is absurd and emotional.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Michigan? Really? What kind of state looks like a mitten? That's not a state, that's a craft project gone wrong. And don't even get me started on the Upper Peninsula — it's like Michigan couldn't commit to being one thing, so it just split itself in half like some kind of geographical commitment-phobe. It's the only state that requires a map just to understand its own borders!

jokes.text: You probably watch Million Dollar Baby and root for the stool. I haven't seen it. Not a big Morgan Freeman guy. I find his voice very grating.
jokes.setup: Leslie making an analogy about Jamm siding with villains
jokes.punchline: Jamm's completely missing the point and insulting Morgan Freeman's voice
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work. Leslie's specific pop culture reference and Jamm's complete misdirection is perfectly written.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're like someone who sides with Voldemort, or Thanos, or the Empire in Star Wars.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Well, those are all fictional villains. I'm a real person making real choices.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Exactly! You're choosing to be evil!

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: I'm not evil. And also, Morgan Freeman's voice is overrated.

jokes.text: I haven't seen it. Not a big Morgan Freeman guy. I find his voice very grating.
jokes.setup: Leslie's Million Dollar Baby reference
jokes.punchline: Jamm's contrarian opinion about Morgan Freeman's universally beloved voice
jokes.explanation: Excellent contrarian take that perfectly fits Jamm's character. Finding Morgan Freeman's voice 'grating' is comedically wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Look, I'm not saying Morgan Freeman doesn't have a nice voice, but let's be honest – it's overrated. Everyone acts like he's God just because he narrates everything. The guy's been in what, a thousand movies? You can't tell me they're all good.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jeremy, Morgan Freeman's voice is literally one of the most beloved things in entertainment.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Exactly. Universally beloved. Which means contrarian me has to hate it.

jokes.text: Wait a second. That's not pizza. Those are calzones! I love calzones!
jokes.setup: Ben's surprise party with food
jokes.punchline: His excitement about calzones, referencing his known obsession
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to Ben's calzone love. Not groundbreaking but satisfying character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Is that... are those calzones? Oh my God, this is the best day of my life. You guys know me so well. Calzones are literally perfect. They're like pizza, but they're also a sandwich. It's the best of both worlds.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I could eat calzones every single day for the rest of my life and die a happy man.

jokes.text: Wait a second. That's not pizza. Those are calzones! I love calzones! We know!
jokes.setup: Ben's coworkers surprise him with a celebration
jokes.punchline: The revelation that they know his calzone obsession and planned accordingly
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Ben's calzone obsession. Shows the accountants actually pay attention to his preferences.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Surprise! We threw you a party!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And we got you a cake!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Oh wow, thank you guys so much!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Actually, that's not a cake.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's twelve calzones stacked on top of each other.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: This is the best day of my life.

jokes.text: I bought that cabin 18 years ago for $2,200. I do not care about the money.
jokes.setup: High offers for Ron's cabin
jokes.punchline: The ridiculously low original purchase price showing how much property values changed
jokes.explanation: Good real estate humor. The massive appreciation and Ron's indifference to profit is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I bought this cabin in 1974 for $400.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wow, that's incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It was a different time. You could get a fully functional cabin, complete with septic system and well water, for the price of a used snowmobile.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well now developers are offering you $3.8 million.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I know. It's outrageous. This cabin is worth exactly what I paid for it: $400. Everything else is just noise.

jokes.text: I'd go up there and take Andy and Champion and just get away from everyone else and look at spiders.
jokes.setup: Ron asks what April would use the cabin for
jokes.punchline: Her perfectly April-like plan to isolate and observe spiders
jokes.explanation: Perfect April response - antisocial, specific, and oddly endearing. 'Look at spiders' is wonderfully weird and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I would use it to isolate and observe spiders in their natural habitat, and document their mating patterns while slowly losing my grip on reality.

jokes.text: I'd go up there and take Andy and Champion and just get away from everyone else and look at spiders. And it's where I'd bury the bodies of that annoying couple after I murder them.
jokes.setup: April explaining what she'd use the cabin for
jokes.punchline: Sweet wholesome activities followed by casual murder plans
jokes.explanation: Perfect April Ludgate dark humor. The tonal shift from sweet to sinister is expertly executed.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'd use the cabin for baking cookies, reading books by the fireplace, taking long walks through nature, and burying bodies where no one would ever find them.

jokes.text: And it's where I'd bury the bodies of that annoying couple after I murder them.
jokes.setup: April continues explaining her cabin plans
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of murder and body disposal as secondary cabin uses
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark April humor delivered matter-of-factly. The casual murder planning is character-appropriate and funny.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: The cabin will be perfect for weekends, hosting friends, and you know, disposing of bodies if needed. I've already scouted good locations in the woods.

jokes.text: I will give you everything in my purse. $8, a bunch of loose cough drops, and Larry's asthma inhaler.
jokes.setup: April making an offer on the cabin
jokes.punchline: Her pathetic financial offer including stolen medical equipment
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor. The random inhaler and April's complete seriousness make it work.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm offering $8,000 and this blood pressure cuff I stole from the hospital.

jokes.text: Larry, this is part of a real estate transaction now. You have no legal claim to it. Sorry, I had no idea.
jokes.setup: Larry recognizing his asthma inhaler
jokes.punchline: April's legalistic argument that theft becomes legitimate through real estate
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic from April. The fake legalism and Larry's polite acceptance is perfectly written.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's my inhaler!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Actually, it's not. You left it in the conference room, which is city property. Anything left on city property for more than 24 hours becomes city property. Therefore, it's my inhaler now.

jokes.text: Jamm is gonna be so pissed when he finds out that you're leaving and your I.O.U.s are worthless.
jokes.setup: Chris made a deal trading future favors for the lockbox
jokes.punchline: The deal is meaningless because Chris is leaving town
jokes.explanation: Good plot payoff. Chris's deception is clever and the irony is satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, you made a deal trading future favors for the lockbox? Chris, you're leaving town in like a week.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I know, but I got the lockbox.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Those favors are worthless now.

jokes.text: Well, it's not the most ethical thing that I've ever done, but on the flip side, Jamm is a big, mean dope.
jokes.setup: Chris acknowledging his deceptive deal
jokes.punchline: His childlike justification for unethical behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Chris's moral reasoning is charmingly simplistic yet practical.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Look, I know it wasn't the most ethical thing to do, but I was desperate. Besides, technically, I didn't lie — I just withheld information.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: And in my defense, everyone does this. It's not like I'm the only one who's ever bent the rules a little bit.

jokes.text: Is this for realskis? This is 100% certified for realskis.
jokes.setup: Ben can't believe he's being offered city manager job
jokes.punchline: Chris's enthusiastic confirmation using silly made-up phrase
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay humor. Chris's earnest use of silly slang is charming but not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, you're offering me the city manager job?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Yes! You're gonna knock it out of the park, Ben. You're gonna be so good at this. It's gonna be epic and legendary and... flippity-floppity-floop!

jokes.text: You can play with one warrior, but it's just not nearly as good. Enjoy. Ben Wyatt.
jokes.setup: Ben leaving his board game for his coworkers
jokes.punchline: Even his farewell note includes detailed game instructions
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and character moment. Ben can't help but give game advice even in goodbye notes.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Even my farewell note includes detailed game instructions. 'Step 1: Remove all pieces from the box. Step 2: Organize by color. Step 3: Read the 47-page rulebook. Step 4: Realize you've made a terrible mistake.'

jokes.text: Let's play. I call ledgerman! No.
jokes.setup: Accountants deciding to play Ben's game
jokes.punchline: Immediate fighting over who gets the score-keeping role
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the ledgerman. Shows the game actually appeals to accountants despite its complexity.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, let's play a game. Who wants to keep score?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'll do it!

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Actually, I think I should keep score.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No way, give it to me.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm keeping the score.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Guys, guys, I clearly have the best handwriting. I should keep score.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hold on, I'm very organized. This is my thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, maybe we should just... not play this game.

tmdb_overview: Leslie comes to terms with her last day in office and plans for the future with Ben. Chris and Ann find out the sex of their child, Andy comes home from London, and Tom looks to the future.
insight: Board game design obsession drives 56 jokes across a surprisingly dense, character-driven half hour.
